agent_output:
```json
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Order cancellation is a critical lifecycle operation in logistics. It intersects fulfillment state, financial settlement, and courier dispatch. A cancellation is not a simple delete — it is a domain event that must respect the physical reality of package handling and the contractual obligations already set in motion.",
    "bounded_context": "Order Management / Fulfillment",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Order", "definition": "A request by a user to have one or more items picked up and delivered to a destination." },
      { "term": "Cancellation", "definition": "A user-initiated request to terminate an order before final delivery. Not a deletion — the order record persists with a 'cancelled' status for audit and billing purposes." },
      { "term": "Order Status", "definition": "The current lifecycle state of an order (e.g., Pending, Confirmed, Dispatched, In Transit, Delivered, Cancelled)." },
      { "term": "Cancellation Window", "definition": "The period during which an order may be cancelled without penalty, determined by the order's current fulfillment state." },
      { "term": "Cancellation Fee", "definition": "A charge applied when cancellation occurs after resources (courier, vehicle) have already been committed." },
      { "term": "Refund", "definition": "The return of payment to the user upon cancellation, which may be full or partial depending on fulfillment state." },
      { "term": "Dispatch", "definition": "The point at which a courier has been assigned and is en route to pick up the package. This is the key inflection point for cancellation policy." },
      { "term": "Cancellation Reason", "definition": "A user-provided or system-categorized reason for the cancellation, required for analytics, dispute resolution, and fraud detection." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Only the user who placed the order (the order owner) may initiate a cancellation. System/admin cancellations follow a separate process.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Allowing arbitrary users to cancel others' orders is a data integrity and authorization violation — the system is corrupt if this is possible.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given user A owns order 123 and user B attempts to cancel order 123, then the cancellation MUST be rejected with an authorization error."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "An order can only be cancelled if its current status is one of: Pending, Confirmed, or Dispatched. Orders that are In Transit, Delivered, or already Cancelled cannot be cancelled.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Cancelling a delivered order is physically meaningless — the goods have changed hands. Cancelling an in-transit order requires a different process (return/recall). Allowing cancellation from these states corrupts order state integrity.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an order with status 'Delivered', when the user requests cancellation, then the system MUST reject the request and return an error indicating the order is not in a cancellable state."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Cancellation of a Pending or Confirmed order results in a full refund with no cancellation fee.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy that could change (e.g., introducing a processing fee). It does not affect data integrity.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an order in 'Confirmed' status with a total of $50, when the user cancels, then the refund amount MUST equal $50 and the cancellation fee MUST be $0."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Cancellation of a Dispatched order incurs a cancellation fee to compensate for committed courier resources. The refund equals the order total minus the cancellation fee.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The fee amount and policy are business decisions that a VP could update. The principle of compensating for committed resources is sound but the specifics are policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a Dispatched order with total $50 and a cancellation fee policy of $10, when the user cancels, then the refund MUST equal $40 and a $10 fee MUST be recorded."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "A cancelled order MUST persist in the system with status 'Cancelled'. It must never be deleted. The cancellation timestamp, reason, and initiator must be recorded.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Audit trail integrity. Financial reconciliation, dispute resolution, and regulatory compliance depend on cancelled orders being permanently recorded.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a successfully cancelled order, when querying the database, then the order record MUST exist with status 'Cancelled', a non-null cancelled_at timestamp, a cancellation_reason, and the cancelling user's ID."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "A cancellation reason must be provided by the user. The system must not allow a cancellation to proceed without one.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a data quality policy. A VP could decide to make it optional. It does not corrupt system state if missing, but it degrades analytics and dispute resolution.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a user submitting a cancellation request with no reason provided, then the system MUST reject the request and prompt the user for a reason."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Cancellation is idempotent: cancelling an already-cancelled order must return success (not an error) but must not create a duplicate cancellation event or trigger a duplicate refund.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Issuing a duplicate refund due to a retry corrupts financial state. This is a data consistency invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an order already in 'Cancelled' status, when the user submits another cancellation request, then the system MUST return success, MUST NOT emit a second CancellationEvent, and MUST NOT initiate a second refund."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "When a Dispatched order is cancelled, the assigned courier must be notified to abort the pickup.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Failure to notify the courier means they perform unnecessary work, arrive at a pickup with no order, and the physical world diverges from system state. This is an operational integrity issue.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a Dispatched order is cancelled, then within the notification SLA a courier notification event MUST be emitted containing the order ID and cancellation status."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A user has an order in 'Pending' status",
      "when": "The user requests cancellation with a valid reason",
      "then": "The order status transitions to 'Cancelled', a full refund is initiated, the cancellation is recorded with timestamp/reason/user, and the user receives confirmation"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A user has an order in 'Confirmed' status",
      "when": "The user requests cancellation with a valid reason",
      "then": "The order status transitions to 'Cancelled', a full refund is initiated, the cancellation is recorded with timestamp/reason/user, and the user receives confirmation"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A user has an order in 'Dispatched' status (courier assigned and en route)",
      "when": "The user requests cancellation with a valid reason",
      "then": "The order status transitions to 'Cancelled', a partial refund (total minus cancellation fee) is initiated, the courier is notified to abort, and the user is shown the fee breakdown before confirming"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A user has an order in 'In Transit' status",
      "when": "The user attempts to cancel the order",
      "then": "The cancellation is rejected with a clear message explaining the order is no longer cancellable and suggesting they contact support for a return/recall"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A user has an order in 'Delivered' status",
      "when": "The user attempts to cancel the order",
      "then": "The cancellation is rejected with a clear message explaining the order has been delivered and directing the user to the returns process"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A user has an order already in 'Cancelled' status",
      "when": "The user attempts to cancel it again (retry/duplicate request)",
      "then": "The system returns success, does not issue a duplicate refund, does not emit a duplicate event, and shows the original cancellation details"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "User A owns an order",
      "when": "User B (not the owner) attempts to cancel it",
      "then": "The cancellation is rejected with an authorization error; no state change occurs"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A user has a cancellable order",
      "when": "The user requests cancellation without providing a reason",
      "then": "The request is rejected with a validation error prompting the user to select or provide a cancellation reason"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "A user initiates cancellation of a Dispatched order",
      "when": "The cancellation confirmation screen is displayed",
      "then": "The user is shown the cancellation fee amount, the refund amount they will receive, and must explicitly confirm before the cancellation is executed"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-10",
      "given": "A cancellation is successfully processed",
      "when": "The order history is viewed",
      "then": "The order appears with 'Cancelled' status, shows the cancellation timestamp, reason, and refund amount (if applicable)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-11",
      "given": "The order status transitions from 'Confirmed' to 'Dispatched' while the user is on the cancellation confirmation screen",
      "when": "The user confirms the cancellation",
      "then": "The system re-evaluates the order's current status at execution time, applies the Dispatched cancellation policy (fee + courier notification), and informs the user of the updated terms — or rejects if the order has moved to a non-cancellable state"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Race condition: order status changes between user viewing the cancel button and confirming cancellation (e.g., Confirmed → Dispatched, or Dispatched → In Transit)", "expected_behavior": "The system must check the order's current status at the moment of cancellation execution, not at the moment the UI was rendered. If the order moved to a non-cancellable state, reject with an appropriate message. If it moved to a state with different terms (e.g., now Dispatched), apply the correct policy and inform the user." },
    { "scenario": "Concurrent cancellation requests (e.g., user double-clicks the cancel button, or two browser tabs)", "expected_behavior": "Cancellation must be idempotent. The first request processes normally. Subsequent requests return success without duplicate side effects (no double refund, no duplicate courier notification, no duplicate events)." },
    { "scenario": "Payment provider is unavailable when refund is initiated", "expected_behavior": "The order transitions to 'Cancelled' immediately (the business decision is made), but the refund is queued for retry. The user is informed that the cancellation is confirmed and the refund is being processed. The system must not leave the order in a limbo state waiting for the payment provider." },
    { "scenario": "Courier has already picked up the package but the system has not yet updated status to 'In Transit'", "expected_behavior": "This is a data staleness problem. The system should honor the recorded state at time of cancellation. If the system says 'Dispatched', the cancellation proceeds under Dispatched policy. Operational reconciliation (package return) is handled by courier ops, not by blocking the user's cancellation." },
    { "scenario": "User cancels immediately after placing the order (within seconds)", "expected_behavior": "If the order is still 'Pending', full refund, no fee. The system must not impose artificial delays or cooling-off periods that prevent immediate cancellation of a Pending order." },
    { "scenario": "Order with a promotional discount or coupon applied", "expected_behavior": "Refund amount is based on the actual amount charged, not the pre-discount price. The coupon/promotion usage should be reversed so the user can reuse it (policy-dependent — flag for PM decision)." },
    { "scenario": "Partial order cancellation (multi-item order, user wants to cancel one item)", "expected_behavior": "The spec says 'cancel their order' — this implies full order cancellation. Partial cancellation (line-item removal) is a separate feature with different complexity (courier re-routing, price recalculation). It should NOT be in scope for this story." },
    { "scenario": "User has multiple orders and cancels one", "expected_behavior": "Only the specified order is affected. No cross-order side effects. Each order's lifecycle is independent." },
    { "scenario": "Cancellation during a system outage or degraded state", "expected_behavior": "If the cancellation cannot be reliably persisted, the system must fail explicitly rather than silently. The user should receive a clear error asking them to retry, not a false confirmation." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue reduction from cancelled orders. Cancellation fees on Dispatched orders partially offset wasted courier costs. Refund processing costs (payment provider fees) may not be recoverable. However, NOT offering cancellation leads to chargebacks, which are significantly more expensive than voluntary cancellations.",
    "operational": "Courier dispatch abort flow must be operationally reliable — a missed notification means wasted fuel, time, and courier trust. Refund queue must be monitored for failures. Cancellation rate becomes a key metric for forecasting demand and courier utilization.",
    "user": "High positive impact on user trust and satisfaction. Users who can easily cancel are more likely to place orders in the first place. Poor cancellation UX (hidden button, confusing fees, failed refunds) is a top driver of negative reviews in logistics apps.",
    "risk": "Fraud risk: users could exploit cancellation to get free services (cancel after pickup but before status update). Abuse risk: high cancellation rates from certain users may indicate non-serious usage. Operational risk: courier notification failures create real-world waste. Financial risk: refund processing failures create support burden and potential regulatory issues.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "End Users (Senders)", "impact": "Gain the ability to cancel orders, improving control and trust. Must understand fee implications for Dispatched orders." },
      { "group": "Couriers", "impact": "Will receive abort notifications for Dispatched cancellations. Must be compensated fairly for wasted trips. Poor notification reliability directly impacts their earnings and morale." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Reduced inbound volume for manual cancellations. Will handle edge cases (refund failures, disputes about fees). Need updated scripts and tooling." },
      { "group": "Finance/Accounting", "impact": "Must reconcile refunds, cancellation fees, and reversed transactions. Need reporting on cancellation rates and refund processing times." },
      { "group": "Product/Analytics", "impact": "Cancellation reasons provide valuable signal for product improvement. Cancellation rate by stage reveals fulfillment friction points." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 45,
    "reasoning": "The spec is a single sentence with no detail on cancellation policy, fee structure, refund rules, state eligibility, or scope. Every business rule above is inferred from standard logistics domain knowledge, not from an actual spec. Critical policy decisions (fee amounts, which states are cancellable, whether reasons are required, coupon reversal policy) are assumptions that need PM confirmation.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Delivered and In Transit orders must not be cancellable — this is a physical constraint",
      "Cancellation must be idempotent — duplicate refunds are a hard financial integrity issue",
      "Order records must persist after cancellation — audit trail is non-negotiable",
      "Authorization: only the order owner can cancel their own order"

      "Whether partial order cancellation is in scope",
      "Coupon/promotion reversal policy on cancellation",
      "Whether Dispatched orders should be cancellable at all, or only Pending/Confirmed",
      "SLA for courier notification upon cancellation"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "This is a logistics/courier context where orders involve physical pickup and delivery",
      "Orders follow a standard lifecycle: Pending → Confirmed → Dispatched → In Transit → Delivered",
      "Payment is collected upfront (not cash-on-delivery), so refunds are relevant",
      "Partial order cancellation (single line items) is out of scope for this story",
      "Cancellation fees exist for Dispatched orders to cover committed courier costs",
      "A cancellation reason is required for analytics and dispute resolution"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not define which order states are eligible for cancellation. The cancellation policy (fees, refund amounts) is entirely unspecified. These are critical business decisions that cannot be safely assumed.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["End Users", "Finance", "Couriers", "Customer Support"],
      "options": [
        "A: Allow cancellation only for Pending/Confirmed (pre-dispatch) — simplest, no courier impact",
        "B: Allow cancellation for Pending/Confirmed/Dispatched with fee for Dispatched — balanced approach",
        "C: Allow cancellation at any pre-delivery state including In Transit — maximum user flexibility, highest operational complexity"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — it balances user flexibility with operational reality. In Transit cancellation requires a recall/return workflow that is a separate feature."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not clarify whether this is full-order cancellation only, or includes partial cancellation (cancelling individual items from a multi-item order). These are fundamentally different features.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["End Users", "Product", "Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "A: Full order cancellation only (this story), partial cancellation as a future story",
        "B: Both full and partial cancellation in this story"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — partial cancellation involves price recalculation, courier re-routing, and inventory management that should be scoped separately."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",

      "affected_stakeholders": ["End Users", "Finance", "Product"],
      "options": [
        "A: Flat fee (e.g., $5) for all Dispatched cancellations",
        "B: Percentage of order total (e.g., 15%)",
        "C: Dynamic fee based on courier proximity to pickup location"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Start with Option A (flat fee) for simplicity and transparency. Dynamic pricing can be iterated on later."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
```
